steps.label: Cylindrical Coordinates
steps.subtitle: Definition
steps.bodyHtml: To convert from rectangular to cylindrical coordinates, we use the conversion $x=r\cos(\theta)$, $y=r\sin(\theta)$ and $z=z$. To convert from cylindrical to rectangular coordinates, we use $r^2=x^2+y^2$,$\tan(\theta)=\frac{y}{x}$ and $z=z$.

steps.label: Special surfaces in cylindrical coordinates
steps.bodyHtml: By fixing one of the variables in cylindrical coordinates, we can obtain some special surfaces. Can you guess what surfaces we obtain by fixing each of the variables?

steps.label: Review of Spherical Coordinates
steps.bodyHtml:
To convert from spherical coordinates to rectangular coordinates:
          $$x=\rho\sin(\phi)\cos(\theta),\quad y=\rho\sin(\phi)\sin(\theta),\quad z=\rho\cos(\phi)$$
          To convert from rectangular coordinates to spherical coordinates:
          $$\rho=\sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2},\quad \theta=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right),\quad \phi=\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2 +z^2}}\right)$$
          Look at the applet to see how the coordinates change as we move the point.

steps.label: Integration in Cylindrical coordinates
steps.bodyHtml:
If the cylindrical region over which we have to integrate is a general solid, we look at the projections onto the coordinate planes. Hence the triple integral of a continuous function $f(r,\theta,z)$ over a general solid region $E=\{(r,\theta,z)|(r,\theta)\in D, u_1(r,\theta)\leq z \leq u_2(r,\theta)\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$, where D is the projection of E onto the $r\theta$-plane, is given by:
          $$\iiint_E f(r,\theta,z)rdrd\theta dz=\iint_D\left[\int_{u_1(r,\theta)}^{u_2(r,\theta)} f(r,\theta,z)dz\right]\,rdr\,d\theta$$
          In particular, if $D=\{(r,\theta)|g_1(\theta)\leq r \leq g_2(\theta),\alpha\leq\theta\leq\beta\}$, then we have:
          $$\iiint_E f(r,\theta,z)r dr d\theta=\int_{\theta=\alpha}^{\theta=\beta}\int_{r=g_1(\theta)}^{r=g_2(\theta)}\int_{z=u_1(r,\theta)}^{z=u_2(r,\theta)} f(r,\theta,z)r dz dr d\theta$$

steps.label: Setting up Triple Integrals in Cylindrical coordinates
steps.subtitle: Examples and change of limits of integration

steps.label: Setting up Triple Integrals in Spherical coordinates
steps.subtitle: Examples
steps.bodyHtml:
Much of the same as said for cylindrical coordinate apply to spherical coordinates, except that the Jacobian is going to be
          $$\rho^2sin(\phi)$$
